Name: ADAPPT 4.0
Organizer: Mukesh Patel School of Technology Management and Engineering (NMIMS), Mumbai
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
ADAPPT 4.0
Eligibility:
Open to inter-college and inter-specialization teams.
Event Overview:
ADAPPT 4.0: The Startup Weekend
is an overnight innovation challenge organized by IETE-SF MPSTME, designed to bridge the gap between industry challenges and entrepreneurial solutions. This unique event brings together students, industry experts, and technology to create real-world startup concepts that address pressing problems across four critical domains.
Unlike traditional hackathons or ideathons, ADAPPT 4.0 follows a Venture Sprint format where participants develop complete startup solutions, combining business strategy with technical implementation. Teams will hear directly from industry experts about actual challenges faced in their organizations, then spend 24 hours developing comprehensive solutions that include both a viable business model and a fully functional website.
Event Details:
Date
: 1st - 2nd November 2025
Venue
: MPSTME | NMIMS, Mumbai
Duration
: 24 hours
Format
: Overnight Venture Sprint
Event Format:
ADAPPT 4.0 follows the
Venture Sprint
methodology, which combines three essential elements of startup creation:
Phase 1: Problem Discovery:
Industry experts from leading companies will present real-world challenges they face in their respective domains. These are not hypothetical problems but actual issues that organizations are struggling to solve. Participants will gain direct insight into industry pain points and market needs.
Phase 2: Business Development :
Teams begin by developing a comprehensive business strategy for their solution. This includes:
Problem analysis and market research
Solution conceptualization and unique value proposition
Business model and revenue strategy
Target audience and go-to-market approach
Competitive analysis and differentiation
Phase 3: Technical Implementation:
Following business planning, teams move into intensive technical development to create a fully functional website that demonstrates their solution. This website serves as the MVP (Minimum Viable Product) showcasing how the startup would operate in the real world.
Phase 4: Presentation and Evaluation:
Teams present their complete venture, business strategy and technical product, to expert judges who evaluate both entrepreneurial viability and technical execution.
Domains
Domain 1: Digital Banking and Finance:
Explore the dynamic world of financial technology where innovation meets security and accessibility. This domain focuses on transforming traditional banking systems through digital solutions that address payment processing, fraud detection, customer experience, and financial inclusion challenges faced by modern financial institutions.
Participants will tackle real-world problems in mobile banking security, seamless payment systems, digital lending platforms, cryptocurrency integration, wealth management automation, and creating accessible financial services for underserved populations. Solutions should combine cutting-edge technology with practical business viability and scalability.
Domain 2: Smart Crop Technologies:
Dive into precision agriculture and sustainable farming technologies that empower farmers with data-driven decision making. This domain addresses critical challenges in modern agriculture including crop optimization, resource management, and connecting farmers to markets through innovative technological solutions.
Key focus areas include crop yield prediction and optimization, pest and disease detection systems, automated irrigation management, soil health monitoring, weather forecasting integration, farm-to-market supply chain solutions, and advisory platforms that make advanced agricultural technology accessible and affordable for farmers of all scales.
Domain 3: Smart Cities & Urban Tech:
Transform urban living through intelligent infrastructure and sustainable city management solutions. This domain tackles the complex challenges of modern urbanization including waste management, energy efficiency, transportation, public safety, and creating livable, environmentally responsible cities for growing populations.
Participants will address problems in smart waste collection systems, traffic management and congestion reduction, energy-efficient infrastructure, water conservation technologies, air quality monitoring, public safety enhancements, digital governance platforms, intelligent parking solutions, and sustainable urban mobility that improve quality of life for city residents.
Domain 4: Cybersecurity and Data Protection:
Navigate the critical landscape of digital security where protecting data and systems is paramount. This domain focuses on defending against evolving cyber threats while ensuring privacy, building trust, and maintaining data integrity in our increasingly connected world.
Challenge areas span threat detection and prevention systems, secure authentication mechanisms, data encryption technologies, privacy protection tools, vulnerability assessment platforms, security awareness solutions, cloud security implementations, IoT device protection, and compliance management systems that safeguard both organizations and individuals from sophisticated cyber attacks.
Rules and Guidelines
General Rules
All team members must be present at registration and throughout the event
Teams must work only on their assigned domain
Teams cannot change members after registration closes
All work must be original and created during the event timeline
Use of pre-existing code templates/libraries is allowed, but the core solution must be built during the event
Plagiarism in any form will lead to immediate disqualification
Teams must adhere to the event timeline and submission deadlines
Organizers' decisions will be final and binding
Code of Conduct
Maintain professional and respectful behavior with all participants, organizers, and judges
No discrimination, harassment, or inappropriate behavior will be tolerated
Respect the venue facilities and equipment
Keep workspaces clean and organized
Follow all safety and security protocols during the overnight event
Any violation of the code of conduct may result in disqualification
Technical Guidelines
Teams must use their own laptops and equipment
Power outlets and Wi-Fi will be provided
Teams are responsible for backing up their own work
Internet access will be available, but teams should prepare for potential connectivity issues
Any external APIs, libraries, or frameworks can be used
Website must be hosted on a publicly accessible platform (Vercel, Netlify, GitHub Pages, etc.)
Development Guidelines
All development must happen during the designated event hours
Teams can use online resources, documentation, and tutorials
External mentorship or help from non-team members is not allowed during development
AI tools (ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot, etc.) can be used for assistance
Teams must be prepared to explain their code and business logic during presentations
Presentation Guidelines
All team members should be present during the presentation
Presentation time limit will be strictly enforced
Teams must demonstrate their working website during presentation
Question and answer session will follow each presentation
No changes to submissions allowed after the deadline
Accommodation and Facilities
Overnight accommodation will be provided in designated classrooms
Separate rest areas for male and female participants
Meals (Dinner, Breakfast) will be provided
Basic first aid facilities available
Security personnel present throughout the event
Disqualification Criteria
Teams will be disqualified for:
Plagiarism or copying others' work
Violation of code of conduct
Failure to meet submission deadlines
Presentation of pre-built solutions
Incomplete team presence
Misconduct or inappropriate behavior

Dymanic Programing:
Dynamic Programming is a problem-solving technique used to solve optimization and counting problems by breaking them into smaller overlapping subproblems and storing their results (usually in a table or array) to avoid redundant computations.
It is mainly used when:
The problem has Overlapping Subproblems, and
The problem exhibits Optimal Substructure
A problem has optimal substructure if the optimal solution of the whole problem can be built from optimal solutions of its subproblems.
(a) Top-Down (Memoization):
Solve recursively.
Store intermediate results (in an array or dictionary).
Avoid re-computation
Typical Steps to Design a DP Solution:
Define the state: Decide what each subproblem represents (e.g., dp[i][j] = result for first i elements and capacity j).
Write the recurrence relation: Express the state in terms of smaller subproblems.
Identify base cases.
Choose memoization or tabulation approach.
Implement and test.

Name: CipherHunt 2.0
Organizer: Chandigarh University (CU), Ajitgarh, Punjab
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
CipherHunt 2.0
CipherHunt 2.0 is an intense, single-round, cybersecurity challenge, organized by Chandigarh University and CypherLock. Designed in a classic Jeopardy-style CTF format, this competition simulates a cutting-edge quantum cyber-crisis, pushing participants to their technical and strategic limits across a broad range of cybersecurity domains.
Theme & Narrative: Quantum Breach: The Last Firewall
The year is 2042. Quantum computing has reached unimaginable heights, shattering traditional cryptographic securities. To protect the new world of instant global communications and quantum-encrypted banking, the world's most advanced defense was built: QuantumFire™ - a quantum-powered global firewall, developed by NeuroCrypt Industries.
The Threat: Q-Havoc's Attack The secretive hacker collective, Dark Qubit, has infiltrated NeuroCrypt. Their objective is to collapse every digital defense and seize control of the global quantum network. They have weaponized NeuroCrypt’s own AI defense platform, creating Q-Havoc - a rogue quantum AI. Q-Havoc is systematically attempting to breach QuantumFire™ , using decoy HoneyPot traps to mislead and slow defenders. Failure means a digital blackout and global chaos.
Your Mission: Elite Cyber Guardians You are part of the top cybersecurity task force assigned to halt the breach. Your mission is urgent: Find and patch every vulnerability before Q-Havoc completes the breach. The team that solves the most challenges, the fastest, and with the fewest HoneyPot triggers will earn the title: Cyber Guardians of the Quantum Era.
Competition Format:
The 30-Hour Final Stand
This is a single, intensive, on-site round.
Event Details:
Mode: Offline (Onsite Participation)
Venue: Chandigarh University
Dates: October 28–29
Duration: 30 Hours Non-Stop
Platform: CTFd – an industry-grade CTF hosting system
Domains Covered (Q-Havoc's Breach Vectors):
Each challenge category represents a unique breach attempt against QuantumFire™:
Web Exploitation
OT
Android
Cryptography
Binary Exploitation (pwn)
Reverse Engineering (rev)
OSINT
Forensics
Evaluation & Eligibility:
Team Size: Mandatory teams of 2–5 members.
Scoring: Dynamic scoring on the CTFd platform. Metrics include flags captured, challenge difficulty, and time of submission.
Tiebreaker: Final flag timestamp + bonus round performance.
Eligibility: Open to all undergraduate/postgraduate students and Individuals passionate about cybersecurity.
Disqualification : Participants must not share flags or solutions during the contest. Any collaboration leads to disqualification. All participants also agree to follow code of conduct of the university and not engage in any attacks against the infrastructure or organisers in any form
Prizes & Recognition:

About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Composio x DevClub IITD x Chirality Labs Agents in Production Hackathon
Submission deadline extended!

https://chat.whatsapp.com/Jvn4bTPRJJAA7ck2HzLMfI?mode=wwt
PROBLEM STATEMENT IS OUT
About the hackathon
The Agents In Production Hackathon challenges participants to build
agentic workflows
that orchestrate complex tasks across multiple tools and services. Each team will use:
Composio Tool Router (Beta) – An experimental feature that discovers, authenticates and executes tools from Composio’s 500+ integrations.
The hackathon’s goal is to build agents in production that can autonomously plan and execute multi‑step tasks using these tools. Projects should demonstrate how an agent can call multiple external apps (e.g., email, calendars, project management, CRM, data stores) to achieve a useful outcome in real-world business settings. Teams are encouraged to explore innovative use‑cases.
Submission Requirements
Each team may submit one project. One team may not have more than 4 members. Submissions must include:
Workflow Code – Your agent implementation in Python, TypeScript, JSON/YAML, or any agent frameworks (Agents SDK, AI SDK, Langchain, etc.).
README/Documentation – A concise README explaining:
What your agent does and the problem it solves.
How to run the project locally (dependencies, installation instructions, environment variables and required credentials).
Why the project is useful or innovative.
If applicable, how to connect third‑party tools using Composio Tool Router (e.g., instruct testers to provide API keys during demo).
Demo Video (1-2 minutes) – A short video showcasing the agent workflow in action. Demonstrate how the agent accepts input, triggers Composio meta‑tools, and produces an output. Include a brief explanation of your approach and highlight any creative or technical innovations. The video is required for judging.
Friction Log – Document challenges encountered (installation issues, SDK limitations, authentication problems, context management, etc.) and how you overcame them. This helps improve future tools and qualifies you for the “Best Friction Log” prize.
.
You just need to provide link to your GH repo as submission + friction logs / linhks of any PRs you raised to composio toolrouter repo (for consideration for dedicated tracks for best PR/friction log etc. )
Judging Criteria
Projects will be evaluated by a panel of judges on the following dimensions:
Criterion
Description
Overall Usefulness and Uniqueness
Does the agent address a meaningful problem? Would people pay for this? How unique is the problem statement?
Creativity & Innovation
Does the project use the Composio Tool Router in novel ways? Does it combine tools in unexpected or effective patterns?
AI Engineering & Agent‑Native Design
Quality of agent architecture: clear agent instructions, proper hand‑offs between agents. Does it leverage Composio meta‑tools effectively (search, plan, manage connections, multi‑execute, remote workbench)?
Completeness & Polish
Does the project work successfully? Consistency of dependencies, requirements etc. Quality of demo? Even if your project is incomplete, submit what you have; partial but well‑documented work is valued.
Adherence to Guidelines
Follow the submission requirements, respect community guidelines and Composio template conventions (see Quality Guidelines below).
Project needs to be executable to be considered for the competition
Prizes include:
₹ 1,00,000 + potential internship opportunities

Everything you need to know about
HackDawn - NIT Arunachal Pradesh
Format:
Strictly Offline & In-Person. This is a high-intensity, on-campus event. Remote participation will not be possible. All team members must be physically present at the NIT Arunachal Pradesh campus for the entire duration.
Venue: Central Library, National Institute of Technology, Arunachal Pradesh.
Team Size:
3 to 5 members. We strongly encourage the formation of inter-college teams.

Process:
Hacking Period:
The 24-hour build period starts at 5:00 PM on November 6th and concludes sharply at 5:00 PM on November 7th.
Project Submission & Judging:
Teams will submit their projects by 5:10 PM on November 7th. Judging rounds and project demonstrations will follow from 8:00 PM on 7th November onwards.
Code of Conduct:
A professional and respectful environment is mandatory. Any form of harassment or misconduct will lead to immediate disqualification and removal from the premises.
Originality: All code, design, and assets must be created during the 24-hour hackathon period. The use of publicly available APIs, frameworks, and libraries is permitted. Any work done prior to the event is prohibited.
Intellectual Property:
Teams retain full ownership of what they build. By participating, you grant the organizers the right to showcase your project for promotional purposes.
Submission:
All projects must be submitted to the designated platform (e.g., GitHub, Devpost) by the deadline. The submission must include a link to the working prototype and a brief presentation deck.
Judging Criteria:
Projects will be judged on four key pillars: Technical Innovation (the quality and complexity of the code), Impact (the potential of the solution to solve a real-world problem), Design & UX (the user-friendliness of the prototype), and Presentation (the clarity and persuasiveness of the final demo).

About Content:
Everything you need to know about
UI Xpress: Design That Speaks
Event Overview
UI Xpress is a team-based
Offline
design and development competition. The goal is to design and build an interactive UI based on a theme announced at the start of the event. The focus is on translating a concept into a tangible user experience, prioritizing flow, visual design, and creativity.
Team Formation
Team Size:
1 to 2 members.
Each team must designate one Team Leader for communication.
Event Format & Rules
Theme:
The theme and problem statement will be revealed on the spot at the start of the event.
Tools:
Participants must bring their own laptops.
Design:
Any design software is allowed (e.g., Figma, Canva, Adobe XD, Sketch).
Development:
Use of frontend technologies (HTML, CSS, JS, frameworks like React, etc.) is permitted.
AI Use:
The use of AI-assisted design or coding tools is permitted.
Originality:
All design and code must be created
during
the event. The use of pre-made templates or previously completed projects is strictly prohibited and will lead to disqualification.
Submission Requirements
Teams must submit
at least one
publicly accessible link to demonstrate their project.
Welcomed submission formats include (but are not limited to):
An interactive, clickable prototype link (e.g., Figma, Canva).
A public GitHub repository link with the source code.
Submitting multiple formats (e.g., both a prototype link and a GitHub link) is encouraged.
Bonus:
Submitting the source design file (e.g., a shareable Figma or Sketch file) is a plus point during judging, as it provides insight into the design process.
Judging Criteria
Submissions will be evaluated based on the following parameters:
User Experience (UX) & Flow:
Ease of navigation, logical flow, and quality of interaction.
Visual Design & Aesthetics:
Color harmony, layout, typography, and overall visual appeal.
Creativity & Innovation:
Uniqueness and originality of the concept and design solution.
Adherence to the Theme:
How well the solution addresses the problem statement.
Technical Implementation :
Quality and functionality of the code.
Results & Awards
The decisions of the judging panel will be final and binding.
Top-performing teams will be awarded.
All participants will receive Certificates of Participation.
Grounds for Disqualification
Use of pre-made templates, existing codebases, or any form of plagiarism.
Failure to submit at least one working project link by the specified deadline.
Professional misconduct or violation of event rules.

The organizer reserves the right to terminate or cancel the event at any time.
Rules may be modified as required by circumstances.
Participants from other colleges may require a fest pass for the offline event.

About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Bytewars
ByteWars (student’s initiative) is a hackathon where innovators, developers, designers, and students unite to tackle real-world problems using cutting-edge technology. Whether you’re a beginner or an experienced coder, ByteWars is your chance to push boundaries, collaborate across disciplines, learn from mentors, and create meaningful solutions.

starts from 28th and ends on 4th nov
whatsapp group :

Problem Statements:
Teams must prepare a presentation (PPT) and choose from six exciting challenges addressing real-world issues:
AI for Email Management:
Design a system that categorizes, prioritizes, and summarizes a user’s inbox to reduce stress and boost productivity.
AI for Fake News Checking:
Build a tool that analyzes articles or social media posts to detect misinformation, rate credibility, and help users validate sources.
AI Agent for Local Services:
Create a system that searches, compares, and books local service providers (plumbers, tutors, gyms, repairmen, etc.) by integrating multiple APIs and sources.
Deepfake & Phishing Defense:
Develop a tool that detects deepfake audio/video scams or phishing attempts in real time before they reach the user.
Education Finder App:
Build a mobile application to help students easily discover courses, universities, and colleges worldwide.
Open Theme Innovation:
Solve any real-world issue in the domains of UI/UX, Web3, or AI, showcasing your creativity.

Name: AGILE - Agriculture Innovation Challenge
Organizer: Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bangalore
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
AGILE - Agriculture Innovation Challenge
AGILE – Agriculture Innovation Challenge:
AGILE (AGriculture Innovation ChalLEnge)
is an online innovation challenge designed to spark creative problem-solving and technological innovation in the field of agriculture.
The event combines hands-on learning, expert mentorship, and competitive challenges across two key tracks:
Machine Learning
and
App Development
, aimed at different participant levels.
Participants will receive access to curated learning resources, expert-led lectures, and mentoring sessions throughout the event.
The goal is to not just host a hackathon but to enable a complete learning journey in agri-tech innovation.
The event will be held completely online.
Event Categories:
ModelForge (Machine Learning Model Challenge):
Participants will develop a machine learning model using a dataset (given by the organisers) related to agriculture (e.g., yield prediction, soil analysis, crop health, etc.).
Teams will have 3 days to train, refine, and submit their models.
The team achieving the most accurate predictions on the evaluation dataset will be declared the winner.
Conducted around the last week of January.
Team Size:
Up to 3 members.
Intended For:
Undergraduate and graduate students.
BlockDev (App Development Contest):
High school students will design a block-based application that provides practical utility for farmers improving agricultural awareness, market access, or productivity.
Apps can be created using platforms such as Blockly, Kodular, Thunkable, or Scratch.
Focus is on creativity, usability, and impact rather than advanced coding.
App problem statements will be released during
Jai Kisan Jai Vigyan Week (Dec 23–29).
Team Size:
Up to 3 members.
Intended For:
High school students (Grades 8–12).
Rules and Guidelines:
All submissions must be uploaded to the specified Unstop platform or repository before the deadline.
Teams must ensure their projects do not include malware, spyware, or offensive/inappropriate material.
Participants must respect copyright and intellectual property laws and use only permitted software, tools, or assets.
Projects must be submitted on time — late submissions will not be considered.
Any prior work or pre-existing code must be clearly mentioned; the majority of development must occur during the hackathon period.
Participants must maintain respect and courtesy toward all team members, judges, and organizers at all times.
Only one submission per team is permitted; resubmissions after the deadline will not be accepted.
All participants must register on Unstop to be eligible.
Teams can seek advice or clarifications from mentors and volunteers but must submit original work.
If two or more teams work on similar ideas, judging will be based on the distinct quality, execution, and innovation of each team’s final product.
Plagiarism will not be tolerated. Any team found to have plagiarized content will be disqualified from the competition.
Submission Rules:
ModelForge:
Submit the trained model, code repository (with clear documentation), and a brief report summarizing your approach and results.
Dataset will be shared at the start of the event.
Models will be evaluated based on predefined performance metrics (e.g., RMSE, accuracy, F1-score).
Submissions must include a README explaining preprocessing, methodology, and dependencies.
BlockDev:
Submit the final app project file or link (as per platform used).
Include a short demo video (max 5 minutes) explaining app functionality and use-case relevance.
Provide screenshots and a one-page description of the app concept and intended agricultural impact.
Eligibility:
ModelForge (Machine Learning Challenge):
Open to undergraduate and postgraduate students from any recognized institution.
BlockDev (App Development Contest):
Open to high school students (Grades 8–12).
Each team may consist of up to 3 members.
Interdisciplinary and cross-institutional teams are welcome.
Learning Resources and Lectures:
AGILE is designed as more than a competition — it’s a guided learning experience. Below is a carefully crafted timeline to foster learning and innovation!
Timeline of Engagement:
31st October:
Registration closes.
Mid-November:
Resources – Part 1 shared (Introductory learning material).
23rd December (Farmer’s Day):
Lecture by Prof. Narahari.
23–29 December (Jai Kisan Jai Vigyan Week):
App Development problem statements released.
Early January:
Resources – Part 2 shared (Advanced guidance for participants).
12th January:
App Development submissions close.
End of January (Pravega):
3-day Machine Learning Challenge.
All lectures and competitions will be conducted online, encouraging participation from across the country.
A lecture by Land Optimizer International Executives — details will be provided soon.
Stay tuned for further updates and detailed information that will be shared soon regarding the event.

DSA Week: Arrays and Strings Mastery
DSA Week is a coding initiative by IEEE TEMS aimed at helping students strengthen their understanding of core Data Structures and Algorithms through a series of Unstop competitions. Each day focuses on a specific topic, allowing participants to learn, practice, and compete in a structured manner. Over the course of the week, participants will tackle challenges from arrays and strings all the way up to dynamic programming and graphs.
About This Event – Arrays and Strings Mastery:
The first event of DSA Week focuses on mastering Arrays and Strings, the foundation of all problem-solving in programming. Participants will solve three curated coding problems that test logical thinking, optimization skills, and implementation efficiency. It’s the perfect starting point to build strong fundamentals for upcoming rounds.

Number of Questions: 3 problems testing fundamental and intermediate DSA concepts.
Duration: 2.5 hours.
Difficulty Level: Beginner to Intermediate.
Evaluation: Based on accuracy, efficiency, and time of submission.
Rules:
Participants must submit original solutions. Any form of plagiarism will lead to disqualification.
Late submissions will not be accepted once the round ends.
Use of AI tools or external code-sharing during the contest is prohibited.
Participants must ensure a stable internet connection during the contest.
The decision of the organizing team will be final and binding.

Name: SFLC.in Hackathon 2025- Defending Digital Rights
Organizer: Software Freedom Law Centre, India
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
SFLC.in Hackathon 2025- Defending Digital Rights
About the Event:
This hackathon aims to address pressing real-world challenges at the intersection of internet freedom, privacy, transparency, and digital rights. Over three days, participants will collaborate in teams to design and develop sustainable, open-source tools guided by mentors from both industry and civil society. The central theme of the hackathon is
Digital Defenders
, with sub-themes focused on
Internet Shutdowns
and
Free Speech
, two of the most urgent and complex digital rights issues in India today. In addition to hands-on development, the event will feature
knowledge sessions, mentorship hours, and community networking
, providing participants with a rich learning environment and partners with valuable visibility.
Prize Breakdown:
1st Prize:
₹15,000
2nd Prize:
₹10,000
3rd Prize:
₹5,000
Additional Rewards:
Swags (T-shirts, stickers, notebooks, etc.) and certificates for all participants
Hackathon Rules:
Eligibility:
Open to all individuals worldwide — students, professionals, activists, designers, and developers.
No prior hackathon experience required.
Participants can join individually or in teams of up to 3 members.
Registration:
All participants must register through the official website.
During registration, participants must either select a project from the curated list or propose their own idea.
Only registered participants are eligible to take part in the hackathon.
Team Formation:
Teams can consist of
1–3 members
.
Cross-disciplinary collaboration is encouraged (developers, designers, policy thinkers, etc.).
Each participant can only be part of one team.
Project Rules:
Projects must align with the central theme:
Defending Digital Rights through Open Source Innovation
.
Focus areas include
Digital Security Training, Free Speech Tracker, Internet Shutdowns Tracking, Transparency & Accountability, and Privacy & Consent
.
Work on a curated challenge or build an approved original idea relevant to the hackathon theme.
Development Guidelines:
All code must be developed during the hackathon period.
Use of open-source libraries, frameworks, and tools is allowed.
Pre-existing projects cannot be submitted, but participants may use publicly available open-source code as building blocks.
Open-Source Licensing:
All submissions must be licensed under
GPL or another recognized open-source license
.
Final submissions must include
source code, documentation, and a demo
.
Code must be uploaded to the official
SFLC.in GitHub/GitLab repository
with proper documentation.
Submissions:
Submissions must be pushed to the official SFLC.in GitHub/GitLab repository.
Late submissions will not be accepted.
Judging Criteria:
Innovation:
Originality of the idea and approach.
Technical Execution:
Functionality, scalability, and code quality.
Usability & Accessibility:
Ease of use and inclusivity.
Impact:
Relevance to digital rights and real-world applicability.
Open-Source Spirit:
Quality of documentation and contribution potential.
Code of Conduct:
Respectful, inclusive, and collaborative participation is mandatory.
Harassment, discrimination, or abusive behavior will result in disqualification.
Participants must adhere to
SFLC.in’s values
of openness, equality, and digital freedom.
Post-Hackathon:
Projects remain open-source and can be developed further after the event.
Promising projects may be highlighted, supported, or connected with collaborators and partners.
By registering, participants agree to follow all hackathon rules.
Learn more about the hackathon here - https://hackathon2025.sflc.in/

Name: Cosmohack1
Organizer: Sourcify community
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Cosmohack1
A 24-Hour Global Virtual Hackathon by SourcifyIN
COSMOHACK 1 is a thrilling 24-hour virtual hackathon organized by
SourcifyIN
, a student-driven tech community built to empower young innovators. The event brings together developers, designers, and thinkers to collaborate, innovate, and build impactful tech solutions for real-world challenges.
About the Hackathon
COSMOHACK 1 is more than a competition — it’s a platform to learn, create, and showcase your skills. Participants will engage in problem-solving, receive mentorship from industry experts, and work on cutting-edge technologies in a dynamic, collaborative environment.
This year’s edition introduces two competitive rounds that test both ideation and execution.
Rounds:
Round 1: Idea Submission (PPT Round)
Participants will submit a PowerPoint presentation describing their proposed project idea, problem statement, and solution approach. ( PPT format will be available during round )
The PPT should include key aspects such as:
Problem statement
Proposed solution and technology stack
Implementation plan
Innovation and expected impact
Top-performing teams will qualify for the final hackathon round.
Round 2: Final Hackathon (Prototype Round)
Qualified teams will participate in a 24-hour virtual hackathon to develop a working prototype of their proposed solution.
Teams will submit:
Source code and documentation
A short demo video showcasing the project functionality
Hosted url ( good to have )
Mentors will guide the teams during this phase.
Tracks:
There will be five thematic tracks focusing on emerging and impactful domains.
Smart Education
Open innovation
Web3 (Project can be made seperately apart from main project or as a main project)
AI/ML
Healthcare
Note:
For the Web3 track, If a team is selected for the second round, they may choose to submit a Web3 project built using the Aptos blockchain — either as their main project ( If it is in round 1 ppt ) or as an additional (second) project.To qualify for this track, Aptos must be used as the blockchain for any Web3-based submission.(Top 10 Full stack Projects based on Aptos will recieve $25 Bounty.)
Eligibility:
Open to all college students across any stream or specialization.
Individuals or teams of up to 4 members can participate.
Inter-college teams are allowed and encouraged.
Inter-specialization teams are also welcome — diversity leads to better ideas!.
Process & Important Dates:
Registration: October 13 – November 10, 2025
Round 1 (PPT Submission): November 16 – November 21, 2025
Approvals roll out: December 8, 2025
Mentoring Sessions: December 18 – 19, 2025
Round 2 (Final Hackathon – Prototype Submission): 19-20th December , 2025
Results Announcement: To be announced
Format:
The hackathon will be entirely virtual and will run for 24 hours.
Teams can use any programming language, framework, or tool of their choice.
Collaboration and communication will take place through online platforms.
All submissions will be made digitally. ( Join our communication channels for updates )
Rules:
All work must be original and developed during the event.
Use of open-source tools or libraries is allowed, but the core implementation must be original.
Teams must follow the Unstop Code of Conduct.
Plagiarism or use of prebuilt solutions is strictly prohibited.
Judges’ decisions are final and binding.
Respect intellectual property and maintain professional ethics throughout the event.
Judging Criteria:
Projects will be evaluated based on:
Innovation & Creativity: Originality of the idea and uniqueness of the approach.
Impact & Relevance: How effectively the project addresses real-world problems.
Technical Feasibility: Implementation quality and technical depth.
User Experience: Ease of use, interface design, and accessibility.
Presentation & Demo: Clarity, explanation, and overall demonstration quality.
Duration:
Round 1 (PPT Round): 5 days window for submission.
Round 2 (Final Hackathon): 24 hours of continuous virtual session.
Why Participate?
Solve real-world challenges.
Collaborate with talented peers from diverse backgrounds.
Receive mentorship from industry experts.
Gain exposure on a global platform.
Compete for recognition and exciting rewards.
Organizer:
SourcifyIN – A student-led tech community empowering learners to innovate, build, and grow.

here.

About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Coding Cascade
Introduction:
Get ready for a coding competition where teamwork and precision are everything. In Coding Cascade, your team of three will face three interconnected coding problems. The output of one coder’s solution becomes the input for the next, forming a continuous chain.
But beware—if one coder fails, the entire chain will collapse in a cascading failure. This challenge tests your coding skills, problem-solving abilities, and your team's coordination under pressure.
Can your team rise to the challenge and complete the cascade flawlessly? Step up, sync up, and prove your coding strength!
Guidelines:
The contest will run for 1 hour and feature 3 problems to solve one for each teammate.
Each team must consist of exactly three members.
The output of one solution becomes the input for the next. If one solution fails, subsequent solutions will become invalid.
Rules:
The contest will run for 1 hour and feature 3 problems to solve.
Each team must consist of exactly three members.
The output of one solution becomes the input for the next. If one solution fails, subsequent solutions will become invalid.
Teams will be given one point for each problem correctly solved given their previous teammate also correctly solves their respective problem, i.e., the chain does not break.
Any participant found engaging in cheating or unfair practices will be immediately disqualified from the contest.
VS Studio Editor will be provided during the contest, and participants may add extensions to it as per their convenience.
Participants are not allowed to use their own laptops during the contest.

Judging Criteria:

Teams that solve more problems with less penalty time will rank higher in the standings.

Beyond the workshops, the Qiskit Fall Fest culminates in an intense, collaborative Hackathon from October 25th to 27th. This is your chance to immediately apply the skills you've learned to real-world quantum problems.
The Hackathon is a continuous sprint where you will form teams, select your challenge tracks, and work with mentors to design and implement innovative solutions using the Qiskit framework and IBM Quantum hardware.
Why Participate?

Harassment or discrimination leads to disqualification.

Report emergencies to the Helpdesk immediately.
Disqualification Rules

Violating the Code of Conduct.

Tampering with event data or setup.
Missing final submission without notice.

Name: Error Stack
Organizer: NIIT University
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Error Stack
Tagline:
It’s not a bug, it’s a feature.
Short Description:
Join the Error Stack challenge, where your debugging skills will be put to the ultimate test! Dive into a series of Python code snippets filled with tricky errors and logical flaws. Your task? Identify, fix, and perfect the code so it passes all the test cases. Can you conquer the bugs and rise to the top of the leaderboard?
Long Description and Overview:
In collaboration with Binary Beasts, Error Stack brings an exciting and intellectually stimulating debugging challenge designed to test your precision, patience, and programming expertise. This competition is a celebration of one of the most crucial skills in software development — debugging. While coding from scratch is one thing, understanding and correcting existing code demands a completely different level of insight and logical reasoning. This event is crafted to evaluate exactly that.
Participants will be provided with multiple erroneous Python code snippets, each representing a unique problem statement. The errors might range from syntax issues and logical mistakes to algorithmic inefficiencies. Your mission is to carefully analyze each snippet, identify the source of the problem, and rewrite the corrected version that performs flawlessly and passes all the hidden test cases.
All the problems will be hosted on HackerRank as part of a structured contest environment. Each question will carry a different point value, determined by its complexity and the level of debugging skills required. Easier problems will test your basic understanding of Python concepts, while the trickier ones will demand strong logical reasoning and code optimization skills. Participants will be ranked on the leaderboard based on their cumulative scores and submission timings.
In this challenge, accuracy is everything — points will only be awarded for solutions that pass all test cases. Partial correctness or incomplete solutions will not fetch any score, ensuring that only the most thorough and precise problem solvers make it to the top.
Whether you’re a beginner looking to strengthen your debugging foundation or a seasoned programmer aiming to prove your mastery, Error Stack promises an engaging experience filled with learning, excitement, and healthy competition. It’s your chance to showcase your problem-solving mindset, attention to detail, and code analysis skills in a competitive setting that mirrors real-world software debugging challenges.
Rules and Guidelines:
Team Size: Individual participation only. Each participant must compete independently.
Participants must submit complete working solutions for each problem. No points will be awarded for partial or incomplete code submissions.
Plagiarism is strictly prohibited. Any instance of copied or AI-generated code will lead to immediate disqualification.
In case of a tie-breaker, the participant who submits first will be ranked higher on the leaderboard.
Use of AI tools such as ChatGPT, Copilot, or other automated code assistants is not allowed. Every code submission will undergo strict plagiarism and originality checks.
Important Dates: Registration Deadline 04 Nov 25, 06:30 PM CUT

Name: DSA Challenge
Organizer: Modern Institute of Technology and Research Centre (MITRC), Alwar
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
DSA Challenge
Welcome to the MITRC Advanced DSA Challenge, a high-stakes, 30-minutes competitive event meticulously designed by MITRC students to push the boundaries of your Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) expertise and dramatically enhance your professional coding skills. This isn't just a contest; it's a rigorous test of your problem-solving maturity under pressure.
Challenge Focus and Difficulty:
This challenge is set at an advanced level, moving beyond fundamental concepts (like basic sorting or list traversal) into complex algorithmic paradigms. Expect problems that require deep knowledge and implementation skills in areas such as:
Graph Theory: Advanced concepts like Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra's, Floyd-Warshall), Minimum Spanning Trees (Prim's, Kruskal's), Network Flow, and strong component analysis.
Dynamic Programming (DP): Problems requiring complex state management, multi-dimensional DP, and optimization techniques.
Advanced Data Structures: Use of specialized structures like Tries, Fenwick Trees (BIT), Segment Trees, Disjoint Set Union (DSU), and various types of balanced search trees.
Computational Geometry and Number Theory: High-level mathematics and geometric techniques integrated into algorithmic solutions.
The difficulty is calibrated to simulate the intensity and complexity encountered in major coding competitions and the most demanding technical interviews. Success will require not just knowing the algorithms, but understanding when and how to apply them optimally.
Supported Programming Languages:
To accommodate diverse backgrounds and allow for optimal performance, the challenge fully supports the following industry-standard languages:
C++: The gold standard for competitive programming. Its high performance and efficient Standard Template Library (STL) are crucial for achieving the necessary speed (low execution time) to pass complex problems with tight time limits.
Python: Valued for its rapid prototyping and clear, concise syntax. While execution may be slower, Python is ideal for quickly developing and verifying complex logic. Note: Participants must be mindful of time complexity, as brute-force Python solutions may time out.
C: Supported for its foundational status and control over memory, offering an excellent environment for testing the core logic and memory efficiency of algorithms.
The 30 Minute Gauntlet:
The time limit for this challenge is 30 minutes  This constraint is intentional. It forces participants to:
Prioritise: Quickly assess problem difficulty and allocate time effectively.
Optimise: Immediately think in terms of the most time-efficient
O
(
⋅
)
solution, as brute-force approaches will likely fail the hidden test cases.
Debug Under Pressure: Rapidly identify and fix errors, distinguishing between logical bugs and implementation errors.
The challenge is scored based on the number of problems solved and the total time taken (with penalties for incorrect submissions). This emphasises both correctness and speed.
Why Participate?
This MITRC-organized challenge offers a unique opportunity to:
Measure Your Readiness: Benchmark your DSA skills against your peers in a competitive, advanced setting.
Master Optimization: Learn to write code that not only works but is also efficient enough to handle large datasets within strict time constraints.
Build Confidence: Successfully tackling these difficult problems builds a robust foundation for technical roles and interviews at top-tier companies.
Prepare to analyze, strategize, and code your way to the top. Your journey to advanced coding mastery starts here!

n the offline phase, teams go through a series of skill-based rounds testing DSA, Aptitude, DBMS, OOPS, Verbal, and Coding before the problem statements are revealed.
From there,
participants progress through ideation, prototype development, and final pitching. Unlike typical eliminations, DAKSH’25 focuses on continuous evaluation and skill growth, with marks accumulated across stages and mentorship sessions conducted to help participants enhance their problem-solving and communication abilities.
The event concludes with live pitching before an expert jury, celebrating not just winners but the future-ready innovators of tomorrow because at DAKSH, we believe in building your future, not just judging your skills.
Eligibility & Team Formation:
Student Status: All participants must be currently enrolled undergraduate or postgraduate students from any recognized college or university.
Team Size: Teams must consist of 6 members. Individual participation is not allowed.
Inter-College Teams: Allowed. Teams can be formed by students from different colleges.
Inter-Specialization Teams: Allowed. We encourage diversity in skills; team members can be from various engineering specializations (e.g., CSE, ECE, Mechanical, etc.).
Registration: All team members must be officially registered through the designated hackathon portal by the stipulated deadline.
Event Format & Process:
Round 1: The Online Technical & Aptitude Challenge:
Mode: Online (Can be conducted at your preferable time between 1st – 10th November).
Purpose: This mandatory shortlisting round assesses individual and collective team strengths in foundational engineering knowledge and problem-solving.
Content: The test will feature sections on:
Coding (DSA - Data Structures & Algorithms): Challenging algorithmic problems requiring efficient code.
Verbal Reasoning: Questions on comprehension, logical deduction, and effective communication.
Aptitude: Quantitative and logical reasoning problems.
Duration: The exact duration will be communicated closer to the test date. All registered team members are expected to participate, and their cumulative performance will determine team shortlisting.
Advancement: Only top-performing teams from Round 1 will be invited to Round 2.
Round 2: The 48-Hour On-Site Hackathon – Innovate | Code | Create | Present:
Congratulations! Having conquered Round 1, your team will now step into the electrifying atmosphere of the 48-hour On-Site Hackathon at GL Bajaj Institute of Technology & Management, Greater Noida. This is the heart of DAKSH 2025 – a continuous, high-energy sprint where your ideas transform from concept to reality.
Round 2 will commence with a crucial initial phase:
Offline Technical Assessment (Initial Phase of Round 2): To further gauge individual proficiency and contribute to your team's overall standing, Round 2 will begin with an on-site written/coding assessment. This test will cover core areas including Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA), Aptitude, Verbal Reasoning, Object-Oriented Programming Systems (OOPS), and Database Management Systems (DBMS).
Scoring Impact: Scores from this assessment will contribute to your team's total evaluation. For each team of 4 members, the best 3 individual scores from this test will be considered and added to your team's overall hackathon score.
No Termination: It's important to note that there will be no termination of teams based on the performance in this initial offline assessment. All teams that qualify from Round 1 will proceed to the full 48-hour build phase immediately after this assessment.
Problem Statement Allocation & 48-Hour Build Phase: Immediately following the offline assessment, teams will be presented with the challenging problem statements. This marks the official beginning of the 48-hour continuous hackathon. Over two intense days and nights, you will collaborate, strategize, and code tirelessly, fueled by innovation and camaraderie, to design and develop your solution.
What to Expect During the 48-Hour Build Phase:
Intense Problem Solving: Teams will choose from a curated list of real-world problem statements spanning cutting-edge domains like Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, Data Science, Web & Mobile Development, Blockchain, IoT, and more.
Continuous Development: Work collaboratively within your team for a full 48 hours, designing, developing, and refining your solution. This immersive experience simulates the agile development cycles of leading tech companies.
Expert Mentorship & Support: Throughout the hackathon, experienced mentors—industry veterans and academic experts—will be available to provide guidance, technical assistance, and invaluable feedback, helping you navigate challenges and optimize your approach.
State-of-the-Art Environment: Operate in a vibrant, fully equipped workspace at GL Bajaj Institute, complete with high-speed internet, necessary infrastructure, and an inspiring atmosphere conducive to breakthrough innovation. Food, refreshments, and comfort facilities will be provided to keep your focus sharp.
Prototype & Pitch: Conclude your 48-hour marathon by presenting a working prototype or proof-of-concept of your solution. This final presentation will involve a demo and a concise pitch to a distinguished panel of judges, highlighting your project's innovation, technical execution, impact, and feasibility.
Rules & Code of Conduct:
Originality: All code and ideas for Round 2 must be original and developed exclusively during the 48-hour hackathon duration. Use of open-source libraries, frameworks, and publicly available APIs is permitted, but the core solution logic and implementation must be new.
Fair Play: Any form of cheating, plagiarism, or unethical practices in either round will result in immediate disqualification of the entire team.
Intellectual Property (IP): Participants retain full ownership of the intellectual property developed during the hackathon.
Respect & Inclusivity: All participants, mentors, organizers, and venue staff must be treated with respect. Discrimination, harassment, or any offensive behavior will not be tolerated.
Venue Rules: Adherence to all rules and regulations of the GL Bajaj Institute campus is mandatory for Round 2.
Organizers' Discretion: The organizing committee reserves the right to modify rules or guidelines at any time to ensure the fairness and success of the event. All registered participants will be notified of any significant changes. The decision of the judging panel and organizing committee will be final and binding.
Skills Required (Across both rounds):
Programming: Proficiency in at least one modern programming language (e.g., Python, Java, C++).
Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA): Strong understanding of fundamental DSA concepts.
Problem Solving & Logical Thinking: Ability to analyze problems and devise efficient solutions.
Technical Aptitude: General knowledge of computer science fundamentals, OOPS, and DBMS.
Communication: Clear verbal and written communication skills (especially for presentations and team collaboration).
Creativity & Innovation: Ability to think outside the box and propose novel solutions.
Teamwork & Collaboration: Effective participation and contribution within a team environment.
Prepare to challenge your limits, collaborate with brilliant minds, and build the future at DAKSH 2025!

About Content:
Everything you need to know about
DATANYX'25
Welcome to Datanyx'25, an exciting 24-hour Datathon organized by AWS Cloud Club MJCET and IEEE SMC MJCET! This event offers an incredible opportunity for you to demonstrate your skills in data analysis, machine learning, software development, and problem-solving while tackling real-world data challenges. We are eager to see what innovative solutions you’ll come up with!
Eligibility Criteria:
Team Size: Teams should consist of 2 to 4 members from the same institution and Inter-college teams are allowed.
Fields of Study: Teams can be formed from various specializations, including AI&DS, AI&ML, CSE, IT, ECE, etc.
Event Structure:
Phase 1 - Shortlisting Round (Online): In the first phase, teams are required to submit a unique problem statement of their own creation, accompanied by a solution abstract with a presentation that aligns with their selected domain. Teams must choose from one of the following domains: Med Tech, Fin Tech, Agro Tech or EdTech. The proposed presentation should reflect originality and innovation within the chosen domain.All submissions must be completed online on the provided ppt template before the designated deadline.
The DATANYX'25 PPT TEMPLATE & THE DOMAIN DESCRIPTION are attached below .
Phase 2 - Finals (MJCET): The top 15 teams from each domain will qualify for the final phase, held in person at MJCET, Hyderabad. Here, teams will be given a new problem statement revealed on the spot along with a sample dataset. They will then have 24 hours to brainstorm, design, implement, build, and submit their final solutions. This phase tests the teams' ability to think critically and collaborate effectively under pressure, turning ideas into impactful solutions within a limited timeframe.
Shortlisted teams are required to pay a participation fee of ₹800.

Healthcare
Because even monsters need good medical care. ‍️

Name: HackVision
Organizer: IEEE NSUT
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
HackVision
HackVision
is a platform where innovation, creativity, and technology come together to address real-world challenges. It brings students, developers, and innovators under one roof to explore and present practical solutions through technology.
Participants will engage with diverse problem statements, applying creativity and technical skills to develop impactful ideas.
More than just prototypes, HackVision emphasizes collaboration, learning, and tangible outcomes. With mentorship and teamwork at its core, the event encourages participants to turn their ideas into meaningful solutions.
Eligibility Criteria & Team Formulation
HackVision 2025 is open to undergraduate and postgraduate students (part-time or full-time) from any discipline in any college in India.
Participants can register in teams with at most 5 members.
Cross-specialization and cross-college teams are allowed.
All the team members are required to verify and confirm their details to form a team.
A student can be a part of only one team.
Any deviation from the above-mentioned guidelines will result in the immediate disqualification of the entire team.
The Participants should be students currently enrolled in a degree/diploma course (part-time or full-time) from any discipline in any college based out of India.
The Participants should not have a criminal conviction or an arrangement or a contract that prevents the Participants from participating in the competition.
The Participants should have proof of age and address proof when requested and must be mandatorily submitted as may be required to confirm eligibility.
Competition Structure
Round 1: Registration
Participants can register in teams, following the guidelines provided on the platform.
Teams must share basic details such as team name, member information, and preferred idea domain.
Early registration is encouraged to ensure a smooth participation process.
Round 2: Idea Submission (25th – 30th October 2025) [Extended to 31st Oct , 12:00 noon]
The official hackathon tracks will be released on 25th October.
Teams must submit their
Idea Presentation (PPT)
between 25th and 30th October.
An optional prototype video or GitHub link may be included to support the concept.
A standard PPT template will be shared to maintain uniformity across submissions.
Selected teams will advance to the next stage based on their submissions.
Round 3: Offline Prototype Showcase (3rd November)
Shortlisted teams will be invited to
Netaji Subhas University of Technology (NSUT)
for the offline prototype showcase on 3rd November .
Each team will present their working prototype before a panel of judges and mentors.
This round will focus solely on demonstration; no additional coding time will be provided at the venue.
Finalists will be selected for the concluding showcase.
Round 4: Final Showcase (Offline Round)
The final round will feature the top teams presenting their projects to a distinguished panel of industry experts, mentors, and faculty members on 3rd November.
Winners will be decided based on innovation, implementation quality, scalability, and overall impact.

One Line One Shot
One Shot One Line - The Coding Challenge
This event is designed to make coding both engaging and accessible by focusing on the power of concise solutions. Participants will be presented with a series of short puzzles where the objective is to produce the correct output using only a single line of code.
The challenges will span across three environments: Python, JavaScript, and Linux shell commands. Each puzzle will display the desired output, and participants must determine the simplest one-liner that generates it. Problems will range from basic arithmetic and string manipulation to slightly more advanced use of built-in functions and logical operations.
The format encourages quick thinking, creativity, and problem-solving under constraints, while reinforcing foundational skills in coding and command-line usage. A live scoreboard or point system can be used to track progress, adding a competitive element to the experience.
By the end of the event, participants will have practiced fundamental programming concepts, gained exposure to multiple languages, and developed confidence in writing concise, functional code.

Name: InnoHacks 2.0
Organizer: Netaji Subhas University of Technology (NSUT), Delhi
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
InnoHacks 2.0
InnoHacks is a 36-hour innovation-driven hackathon designed to bring together creative minds, developers, and problem-solvers to build real-world solutions using technology. Participants will collaborate, ideate, and create impactful prototypes around emerging technologies like AI, sustainability, and digital transformation.
Software Track
Rural Healthcare
Develop a mobile app for tracking and reporting vector-borne diseases in rural communities, with features for photo uploads and offline reporting.
Build a platform that allows community health workers to schedule, record, and analyze immunization drives in villages, with automated SMS reminders.
Create a telemedicine system for rural clinics that integrates with local pharmacies to manage prescriptions and drug stocks while supporting regional languages.
Education
Develop an adaptive learning app that recommends remedial content to rural students based on periodic assessments, supporting subjects in vernacular languages.
Build a web platform where teachers in government schools can share custom-designed lesson plans and download peer-reviewed resources for remote learning.
Propose and implement software for tracking educational grants and resources disbursed to rural schools; generate transparency reports for local stakeholders.
Waste Management
Create an app for panchayats to log daily waste collection and generate predictive analytics for route planning of sanitation workers in rural settlements.
Design a digital reporting tool for citizens to record illegal dumping with geo-tagged photographic evidence and escalate complaints to local authorities.
Governance
Build a dashboard for rural administrators that links real-time project completion data from multiple government schemes, highlighting discrepancies and overdue works.
Develop an analytics-driven feedback system that lets citizens anonymously rate public services, with data forwarded to district-level decision-makers.
Hardware Track
Rural Healthcare
Design a low-cost, portable diagnostic toolkit for community health workers to conduct basic blood and urine tests; automatically upload results to a central server via GSM.
Prototype a wearable monitoring patch for elderly villagers, which records vital health data and sends alerts to a remote caregiver dashboard.
Education
Engineer a solar-powered smart board for rural schools that supports offline digital lessons and interactive exercises using locally-stored content.
Build low-power IoT attendance devices for students in remote schools, syncing data via mobile networks for government audit purposes
Waste Management
Prototype sensor-enabled bins that alert local collection centers when full, optimizing pick-up schedules for sanitation vehicles in small towns.
Develop a simple mechanical sorting device (manual or powered) for dry and wet loads in gram panchayat collection yards to minimize landfill loads.
Governance
Propose a robust biometric system for village-level public distribution shops, ensuring seamless offline operation and tamper-proof authentication.
Build a tamper-evident lock sensor system for village storehouses (e.g., ration or fertilizer) that logs every access event and reports anomalies to authorities.

Teams can use any open-source tools, frameworks, or APIs.
Shortlisted teams will be notified via email for the final prototype round.
Mentorship sessions will be provided during the on-site hackathon.
All decisions by the judging panel will be final and binding.

How does your solution address the problem?
Direct connection to problem statement
What makes your approach unique/innovative?
Key differentiators
What current solutions exist and why are they insufficient?
Gap analysis
How will users interact with your solution?
APP/Web
4. Technical Approach & Feasibility (60 seconds)
What technologies/tools will you use?
Tech stack overview
What is your development approach?
High-level architecture or methodology
What can you realistically build in 8-10 hours?
Scope for the hackathon
5. Impact & Market Potential (30 seconds)
What impact will your solution have?
Quantify if possible
How scalable is your solution?
Growth potential
What is the potential market/user base?
Size and reach
How could this be monetized/sustained?
Business viability (if applicable)
Additional Guidelines for Participants
Video Production Tips:
Keep it engaging:
Use visuals, mockups, or simple animations if possible. Editing of the video is allowed
Practice timing:
Rehearse to stay within the 5-minute limit
Clear audio:
Ensure all speakers are audible
Conditions:
Face isn’t compulsory only sound with your ppt screen is more than enough
Content Strategy:
Be specific, not generic:
Avoid vague statements
Show, don't just tell:
Use examples, scenarios, or use cases
Be realistic:
Don't overpromise what you can deliver in one hackathon session
Evaluation Criteria Hints:
Your video should demonstrate:
Solution innovation
and feasibility
Presentation skills
and communication clarity
Potential impact
and scalability
Common Mistakes to Avoid:
Spending too much time on introductions
Being too technical without explaining the "why"
Not connecting solution back to the original problem
Unrealistic scope for an 8-10 hour hackathon
Poor time management in the video structure
Not practicing the presentation beforehand
This format ensures participants cover all essential aspects while staying within the time limit and preparing them for a successful hackathon experience.

About Content:

Beyond Hack:- 24 Hour National Level Hackathon
Theme:
Innovation begins where ordinary thinking ends.
Overview:
Code Beyond Limits
challenges students to uncover problems that are often overlooked and to create solutions that are unconventional, meaningful, and impactful. The event inspires participants to think differently, push boundaries, and design technology that transcends ordinary solutions.
Tracks & Problem Statement:
HealthTech:
Overview:
Discover hidden challenges in health and wellness. Design creative solutions that enhance physical, mental, or community well-being in unconventional ways.
Problem Statement 1:
Develop a solution that improves everyday wellness and lifestyle, such as fitness, mental well-being, or work-life balance, using innovative technology.
Problem Statement 2:
Create a tool or system that detects, prevents, or manages overlooked health conditions in communities, schools, or workplaces, focusing on early intervention and impact.
EduTech:
Overview:
Rethink education and learning. Build solutions that make education engaging, accessible, and impactful by addressing unnoticed gaps in learning systems.
Problem Statement 1:
Create a solution that addresses gaps in learning engagement or accessibility, making education more inclusive and effective.
Problem Statement 2:
Innovate a platform or tool that transforms a routine educational process into an interactive and impactful experience.
FinTech:
Overview:
Drive financial innovation by addressing hidden challenges. Design tools or platforms that simplify, secure, and improve financial experiences in new ways.
Problem Statement 1:
Develop a solution that tackles hidden financial challenges faced by students, individuals, or small communities.
Problem Statement 2:
Design a tool that enables smarter, more informed financial decision-making, beyond traditional banking solutions.
GreenTech:
Overview:
Develop sustainable solutions for overlooked environmental challenges. Create technology that makes sustainability practical, effective, and community-driven.
Problem Statement 1:
Develop a digital solution that transforms everyday waste, emissions, or energy usage data into actionable insights for sustainable living.
Problem Statement 2:
Design a community-driven platform that motivates collective action for environmental change through gamification or real-time tracking.
InnoTech:
Overview:
A free track for bold, boundary-pushing ideas that don’t fit existing categories. Participants can merge domains or explore unconventional challenges.
Problem Statement 1:
Explore unconventional problems that don’t fit existing categories and create bold, imaginative solutions.
Problem Statement 2:
Combine multiple technologies or domains to develop a project that pushes the boundaries of innovation and impact.

is an adrenaline-packed cybersecurity challenge where participants put their hacking and problem-solving skills to the test. From cracking codes to uncovering hidden vulnerabilities, every round pushes competitors to think sharper and move faster. With mind-bending puzzles, surprise twists, and a race against time, the event is more than just a competition—it’s an electrifying journey into the world of ethical hacking and cyber defense.
Event Specific Rules:-
Structure & Progression:
The Event Has 3 Levels; Each Level Contains Progressively Harder Challenges.
Each Team Starts with Level 1 and Must Complete It to Unlock Level 2, Then Level 3.
The Output of A Challenge in One Level Serves as The Input for The Next Level.
Level Descriptions & Marking:
Level 1 — Steganography:
50 Points
Analyze an Image/audio File to Extract Hidden Data (e.G., Using Steghide).
Output: Extracted String (url/encrypted Message) Required for Level 2.
Level 2 — Cryptography:
100 Points
Use Level 1 Output to Decrypt an Encoded Message (e.G., Caesar, Xor).
Output: Decrypted String Revealing Credentials/key for Level 3.
Level 3 — Buffer Overflow / Exploitation:
200 Points
Use Credentials from Level 2 to Access/exploit a Vulnerable Binary and Obtain the Final Flag.
Bonus (fastest Completions):
Fastest Level 1: +10 Points
Fastest Level 2: +20 Points
Fastest Level 3: +50 Points
Hints & Penalties:
Teams May Request up To
3 Hints per Challenge Level
.
Hint Costs (deducted from Team Score):
Level 1 Hint = -5 Points
Level 2 Hint = -10 Points
Level 3 Hint = -20 Points
Challenge Switching & Restrictions:
Once a Team Starts a Level, They Cannot Switch Back to A Previous Level.
Allowed Tools Include: Wireshark, Steghide, Ghidra, Gdb, and Basic Cl Utilities.
Buffer Overflow/exploitation Tasks Expected to Use C/python Where Applicable.
Scoring Summary (per Team):
Level 1: 50 Pts, Level 2: 100 Pts, Level 3: 200 Pts + Fastest Bonuses – Hint Penalties.
Total Time Available for All Levels:
3 Hours
.
Tie-Breaker:
If Scores Tie, the Team that Completed the Highest Difficulty Level in The Shortest Time Wins.

Name: Mineral Forecasting
Organizer: Indian Institute of Technology, Indian School of Mines (IIT ISM), Dhanbad
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Mineral Forecasting
Teams are challenged to develop a comprehensive data analytics solution that analyzes India's Export-Import (EXIM) data for Copper, Lithium, and Graphite to support strategic decision-making and enhance mineral security.
Core Objectives:
EXIM Data Analysis:
Analyze historical trade data for India's 30 critical minerals using data from the Directorate General of Commercial Intelligence and Statistics (DGCI&S)
Identify import-export patterns, trade volumes, values, and key trading partners
Assess India's dependency ratios for each critical mineral.
Examine trade balance trends and their economic implications.
Apply ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) to compare trade performance across different mineral categories, time periods, or trading partners.
Trend Analysis:
Conduct a temporal analysis of critical mineral trade flows from 2017-18 to the present.
Identify seasonal patterns, cyclical trends, and structural changes in trade dynamics.
Analyze price variations and their correlation with global market conditions
Machine Learning-Based Forecasting:
Develop predictive models to forecast future import/export volumes and values for critical minerals
Implement time series forecasting techniques such as ARIMA, LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory), or hybrid ARIMA-LSTM models.
Generate short-term (6-12 months) and medium-term (2-3 years) forecasts
Validate model performance using appropriate evaluation metrics (MAE, MAPE, RMSE, R² score)
State-Level Mineral Extraction Mapping (Optional Advanced Component):
Explore the feasibility of using GST data to identify state-level mineral extraction patterns.
Cross-reference GST collections under mineral-specific HSN codes (Chapter 25-27) with known mineral-producing states.
Analyze state-wise contribution to mineral production using available data from the Indian Bureau of Mines (IBM) and the Geological Survey of India (GSI).
Note: Direct state-level extraction mapping from GST data alone may have limitations, as minerals extracted in one state are often processed or value-added in another state.
Critical Mineral Mapping for India:
Create a comprehensive mineral mapping framework that integrates:
Current domestic reserves and production capacity
Import dependency ratios by mineral
Major source countries for imports
Potential domestic exploration areas identified by GSI's 1,200 exploration projects.
Strategic recommendations for reducing import dependence
Expected Deliverables:
Comprehensive Analysis Report containing:
Historical EXIM trend analysis for Copper, Lithium, and Graphite.
Identification of high-dependency minerals requiring urgent attention.
ML model comparison and performance evaluation.
Forecasts with confidence intervals for Copper, Lithium, and Graphite.
Critical Mineral Mapping Dashboard featuring:
Import dependency matrix by mineral
State-wise production mapping (where feasible)
Trade partner network analysis
Forecasted demand-supply gaps
Working MVP:
A live, working MVP interface is required, enabling non-technical users to explore trends, run forecasts, and view statistical results with intuitive controls and filters.
Data filters for mineral, time range, trade flow (import/export), country, port, and state (where available), etc.
Toggle views for volumes, values, unit prices, and dependency ratios with dynamic charts and tables.

Participants will receive the exact problem statement through Unstop. Teams must develop and submit their predictive model along with a brief report outlining their approach.
Round 2: Mentor Assignment
Top teams will be asssigned a mentor to further refine their models.
Round 3: Presentation Round
Teams selected for round 2 will present their ideas in front of a panel of judges.

Name: Price & Premium
Organizer: Fintech Club, IIT (ISM) Dhanbad
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
Price & Premium
Price & Premium is a data-driven insurtech competition that challenges participants to build machine learning solutions for portfolio optimisation specifically tailored to Gen Z customers. The goal is to leverage data science, behavioural modelling, and financial strategy to dynamically optimise insurance portfolios, aiming to boost returns, reduce claims, and personalise products.
Eligibility:
Open to all undergraduate and postgraduate students across India.
Teams of 2–4 members are allowed.
Cross-disciplinary teams (e.g., Computer Science + Finance) are highly encouraged.
Cross-college teams are allowed.
Round 1: ML Solution Submission:
Participants will be provided with a curated dataset simulating Gen Z insurance behavior, claims, and lifestyle signals.
Select a problem sub-theme (e.g., risk scoring, product bundling, premium pricing, capital allocation).
Build a machine learning model or agent-based system that addresses the chosen sub-theme.
Technical Report Requirements (Max 8–10 slides or 5 pages):
The report must provide a comprehensive overview of the solution:
Problem understanding and chosen approach.
Data preprocessing and feature engineering methods.
Model architecture and underlying rationale.
Evaluation metrics and detailed results.
Business impact and discussion of scalability.
Round 2: Final Presentation:
Shortlisted teams will present their finalized ML solution to a panel of judges composed of insuretech professionals, data scientists, and faculty members.
Presentation Time: 7–10 minutes.
Q&A Session: 2–3 minutes.
Rules & Regulations:
All models, code, and reports must be original and backed by reliable data sources.
Plagiarism or the use of pre-trained models without proper attribution will lead to immediate disqualification.
Submissions must demonstrate a balance of technical rigor and business relevance.
The use of agents (e.g., reinforcement learning, multi-agent systems) is permitted and encouraged.
Teams must strictly adhere to the presentation time limits in Round 2.
The decision of the judges and organizers will be final and binding.
Evaluation Criteria:
Submissions will be scored on the following weighted criteria:
35%:  Complexity, correctness, and innovative use of ML techniques.
30%:  Potential profitability, market scalability, and real-world usefulness to an insurtech company.
20%:  Quality of the technical report and the clarity of the final presentation (Round 2).
15%:  Novelty of the solution and the insightful incorporation of non-traditional data sources.

Name: SiliconSprint
Organizer: National Institute of Technology, Karnataka & R V College of Engineering, Bangalore
About Content:
Everything you need to know about
SiliconSprint
Event Overview:
SiliconSprint is the National-level Electronics hackathon, which is a monumental collaboration between IEEE NITK Surathkal and IEEE RVCE Bangalore.
This premier Virtual event calls upon the brightest innovative designers and problem-solvers from across the nation to tackle cutting-edge, real-world challenges in electronics.
SiliconSprint is structured around two core domains, featuring unique problem statements based on critical industry themes:
Analog Domain
Digital Domain
Participants will dive into an intense 24-hour period of design, simulation, and collaboration.
As a premier Electronics hackathon, SiliconSprint offers invaluable hands-on learning experiences and unparalleled opportunities to network with industry experts and academics.
Get ready to design, simulate, and innovate beyond the boundaries.
Rounds Overview & Timeline:
Round 1: Online Assessment
Date: November 7, 2025
Time: 6:00 PM - 7:00 PM
Format: A 60-minute online MCQ-based test covering fundamentals in Digital Electronics, Analog Electronics, and General Aptitude.
Outcome: Shortlisted teams based on this assessment will advance to the main hackathon round.
Round 2: Main Hackathon
Problem Statements Release:
Date: November 8, 2025
Time: 11:00 AM (Noon)
Event: The main event begins with an inaugural talk by professionals from Tenstorrent.
Action: Following the talk, problem statements will be released to the shortlisted teams.
Problem Statements Selection:
Date: November 8, 2025
Time: 2:00 PM
Action: A Google Form will be circulated, allowing each team to officially select one problem statement they will commit to solving for the remainder of the hackathon.
Progress Presentation (Mid-Review):
Date: November 8, 2025
Time: 9:00 PM - 11:30 PM
Action: All participating teams will present their progress, methodology, and initial designs to a panel for crucial mid-point feedback. Ranking will be clearly based upon the approach, innovation, teamwork, and presentation skills. Usage of AI tools will lead to immediate disqualification from the event.
Final Submission Deadline:
Date: November 9, 2025
Time: 12:00 PM (Noon)
Platform: The submission portal on Unstop closes. All final design files, simulation results, reports, and presentation materials must be submitted by this deadline.
Round 3: Final Presentation
Date: November 9, 2025
Time: 5:00 PM onwards
Event: Finalist teams will present their completed projects and give a live demonstration to a jury composed of external industry experts and academics. Winners will be decided based on this final evaluation.
